narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of failing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 29.30, Year 1 school decile ranking of 3rd out of 10, Year 3 decile of 2nd out of 10, and your plan to attend full-time—and calculated a 75.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 academic decile, your Year 1 decile, and your LSAT score, in that order of impact. Your Year 3 decile ranking of 2—well below the typical student's 5.5—had the strongest push toward predicting failure, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who passed the bar. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 3, lower than the average of about 5.7, reinforced this concern by highlighting early struggles that often correlate with bar exam challenges. On a more positive note, your LSAT score, though below the dataset average of 36.7, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward success, showing some standardized testing strength amid the other red flags. Overall, your academic deciles stood out as unusually low compared to typical admitted students, outweighing other elements like your full-time status, which is common among 93% of applicants. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 is also below average, it played a lesser role here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements—but the model saw patterns in your grades that matched historical bar failures. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after these steps could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors to law school. You're capable of turning this around with deliberate effort.
